section.title: Amaziah and Jeroboam II
section.sectionRationale: Amaziah of Judah defeats Edom but then, made proud by victory, foolishly provokes Israel's Jehoash into a war that humiliates Judah, breaches Jerusalem's wall, and ends in his own assassination. Jeroboam II then reigns over Israel with unprecedented territorial success, not because of his righteousness but because the LORD had compassion on suffering Israel and had not yet spoken of blotting out their name.
section.thesis: Pride following genuine victory leads Amaziah into ruinous, self-inflicted defeat, while Jeroboam II's remarkable success under continued idolatry shows that national prosperity is never proof of divine approval but can flow purely from the LORD's undeserved mercy toward a people He has not yet given up.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Amaziah's victory over Edom and his proud challenge to Israel, verses 1 to 14; the deaths of Amaziah and Jehoash and Azariah's succession, verses 15 to 22; and Jeroboam II's expansive reign over Israel, verses 23 to 29.

units.label: Amaziah's victory and his proud challenge

verses.text: In the second year of the reign of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz over Israel, Amaziah son of Joash became king of Judah.
He was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem twenty-nine years. His mother's name was Jehoaddan; she was from Jerusalem.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, but not as his father David had done. He did everything as his father Joash had done.
Nevertheless, the high places were not taken away, and the people continued sacrificing and burning incense on the high places.
As soon as the kingdom was firmly in his grasp, Amaziah executed the servants who had murdered his father the king.

Yet he did not put the sons of the murderers to death, but acted according to what is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, where the LORD commanded: "Fathers must not be put to death for their children, and children must not be put to death for their fathers; each is to die for his own sin."
Amaziah struck down 10,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt. He took Sela in battle and called it Joktheel, which is its name to this very day.
Then Amaziah sent messengers to the king of Israel Jehoash son of Jehoahaz, the son of Jehu. "Come, let us meet face to face," he said.

But Jehoash king of Israel replied to Amaziah king of Judah: "A thistle in Lebanon sent a message to a cedar in Lebanon, saying, 'Give your daughter to my son in marriage.' Then a wild beast in Lebanon came along and trampled the thistle.
You have indeed defeated Edom, and your {{lev|heart}} has become {{gaon|proud}}. Glory in that and stay at home. Why should you stir up trouble so that you fall, you and Judah with you?"
But Amaziah would not listen, so Jehoash king of Israel advanced. He and King Amaziah of Judah faced each other at Beth-shemesh in Judah.
And Judah was routed before Israel, and every man fled to his home.

There at Beth-shemesh, Jehoash king of Israel captured Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Joash, the son of Ahaziah. Then Jehoash went to Jerusalem and broke down the {{chomah|wall}} of Jerusalem from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate, a section of four hundred cubits.
He took all the gold and silver and all the articles found in the house of the LORD and in the treasuries of the royal palace, as well as some hostages. Then he returned to Samaria.

units.label: The deaths of the two kings

verses.text: As for the rest of the acts of Jehoash, along with his accomplishments, his might, and how he waged war against Amaziah king of Judah, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Jehoash rested with his fathers and was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel. And his son Jeroboam reigned in his place.
Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah lived for fifteen years after the death of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel.
As for the rest of the acts of Amaziah, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And conspirators plotted against Amaziah in Jerusalem, and he fled to Lachish. But men were sent after him to Lachish, and they killed him there.
They carried him back on horses and buried him in Jerusalem with his fathers in the City of David.
Then all the people of Judah took Azariah, who was sixteen years old, and made him king in place of his father Amaziah.
Azariah was the one who rebuilt Elath and restored it to Judah after King Amaziah rested with his fathers.

units.label: Jeroboam II's expansive reign
units.note: the LORD had not yet spoken of blotting out the name of Israel

verses.text: In the fifteenth year of the reign of Amaziah son of Joash over Judah, Jeroboam son of Jehoash became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria forty-one years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and did not turn away from all the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit.

This Jeroboam restored the boundary of Israel from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah, according to the word that the LORD, the God of Israel, had spoken through His servant Jonah son of Amittai, the prophet from Gath-hepher.
For the LORD saw that the affliction of the Israelites, both slave and free, was very bitter. There was no one to help Israel,
and since the LORD had said that He would not blot out the {{shem|name}} of Israel from under heaven, He saved them by the hand of Jeroboam son of Jehoash.

As for the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, along with all his accomplishments and might, and how he waged war and recovered both Damascus and Hamath for Israel from Judah, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Jeroboam rested with his fathers, the kings of Israel. And his son Zechariah reigned in his place.

lev.gloss: Heart, the seat of will and inner disposition. Jehoash's blunt warning, your heart has become proud, correctly diagnoses that Amaziah's real crisis after victory was not military but spiritual, a heart swelling with self-confidence right before it was humbled by defeat.

chomah.gloss: Wall, the fortified defense of a city. That Israel's king breaches Jerusalem's own chomah, a four hundred cubit section, is a humiliating reversal for Judah, whose proud king had sought this very confrontation, showing how quickly self-generated conflict can expose real vulnerability.

gaon.gloss: Pride, majesty, or exaltation, related to the same root behind Jehoash's warning that Amaziah's heart had become proud. Pride here functions as the hinge of the narrative, turning a genuine God-given victory into a self-inflicted catastrophe through overreach.

shem.gloss: Name, here standing for Israel's continued existence and identity as a nation. The LORD's stated unwillingness to blot out Israel's shem, even under Jeroboam II's idolatrous reign, shows that national preservation here rests entirely on God's compassion and prior word, not on any merit of king or people.

basic.context: Amaziah of Judah begins with a mixed but generally positive reign, carefully applying the law of Moses rather than punishing his father's murderers' children, and winning a real victory over Edom. Yet success swells his heart with pride, and he foolishly challenges Israel's King Jehoash to battle despite Jehoash's wise warning. Judah is routed, Jerusalem's wall is breached, the temple and palace are plundered, and Amaziah's own subjects eventually assassinate him. Meanwhile Jeroboam II reigns over Israel for over four decades with striking territorial success, restoring Israel's boundaries as the prophet Jonah had foretold, not because of any righteousness, since he continues Jeroboam I's idolatry, but purely because the LORD had compassion on suffering Israel and had not yet decreed their national end.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting the reigns of specific kings; it is not a general promise that pride always leads to military defeat or that national prosperity signals divine approval. What endures is the sobering pattern that pride following genuine success often precedes a humbling fall, and the crucial reminder that visible national or personal success can flow from God's undeserved mercy rather than being evidence of righteousness.
basic.originalLanguages: Jehoash rightly diagnoses that Amaziah's lev, heart, has grown proud after real victory, a gaon, pride, that leads him to provoke a war he cannot win, resulting in Jerusalem's own chomah, wall, being breached by the very rival he challenged. Meanwhile Jeroboam II's remarkable success flows purely from the LORD's determination not to blot out Israel's shem, name, from under heaven, a mercy rooted in God's own prior word rather than the king's continued idolatry.
basic.typology: Amaziah's pride following victory, leading to a devastating and humbling fall, previews the New Testament's warning that pride goes before destruction, while Jeroboam II's undeserved national success anticipates the truth that God's kindness, wherever it is shown, is meant to lead to repentance rather than complacency.
basic.christ: Amaziah's proud self-assertion after a genuine victory, provoking a fight he need not have started, stands in stark contrast to Christ, who, despite possessing all authority, humbled Himself rather than asserting His rights, Philippians 2:6-8, showing that true strength is displayed not in provoking unnecessary conflict but in humble submission to the Father's will.
basic.god: God grants real, undeserved success even to an idolatrous king like Jeroboam II, purely out of compassion for a suffering people and faithfulness to His own prior word, not because success signals His approval of the king's ways. He also allows pride following genuine blessing to lead naturally toward a humbling fall, as with Amaziah, showing His moral order operates even when it is not immediately visible to the proud in the moment of their triumph.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16:18 states plainly that pride goes before destruction, the exact pattern displayed in Amaziah's downfall. Jonah 1:1 identifies the same prophet Jonah whose word of restoration for Israel is fulfilled here, showing his ministry extended beyond the well-known Nineveh episode. Romans 2:4 teaches that God's kindness is meant to lead to repentance, a truth Jeroboam II's undeserved prosperity should have prompted but evidently did not.
basic.oneStory: Amaziah's proud overreach after real victory and Jeroboam II's undeserved national success both testify that human triumph is never a simple measure of righteousness, but flows from a deeper reality, the LORD's own moral order and covenant mercy, previewing the gospel truth that all blessing ultimately depends on God's grace rather than human merit, fully displayed in Christ's humble obedience rather than self-exalting pride.

context.literary: The chapter is structured around a deliberate contrast between Amaziah's genuine but pride-corrupted success in Judah and Jeroboam II's remarkable but undeserved success in Israel, framing national prosperity throughout as morally ambiguous evidence rather than straightforward proof of divine favor.
context.historical: The Valley of Salt and Sela, likely the Edomite stronghold later known as Petra, mark real geographic locations of Amaziah's Edomite campaign. Jeroboam II's reign, corroborated by extrabiblical references to Israel's territorial extent in this era, represents the northern kingdom's political and economic high-water mark shortly before its rapid decline and eventual fall, described later in chapters 15 and 17.
context.canonical: This chapter's brief mention of Jonah son of Amittai identifies the same prophet who later resists his call to preach to Nineveh in the book of Jonah, confirming his ministry included this earlier, favorable word to Israel as well, and Jeroboam II's reign forms the prosperous but spiritually hollow backdrop against which the prophets Amos and Hosea soon minister.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that pride following genuine success can lead directly to a humbling downfall, as with Amaziah, and that even remarkable national prosperity, as under Jeroboam II, can flow purely from God's compassion and prior word rather than serving as proof of righteousness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates the specific reigns and outcomes of two kings; it does not prescribe that every proud challenge will end in military defeat or that every season of national success indicates continued sin. What endures is the wisdom of taking pride seriously as a spiritual danger and refusing to equate visible prosperity automatically with divine approval.

debates.question: Why does the text attribute Jeroboam II's remarkable territorial success to the LORD despite explicitly condemning his continued idolatry, verses 24 to 27?

views.label: Pure, undeserved compassion rooted in God's prior covenant word
views.case: Verse 26 explicitly grounds Israel's deliverance in the LORD seeing their bitter affliction and having no other helper, and verse 27 ties it directly to His prior determination not to blot out Israel's name, making the success a display of unmerited divine mercy rather than any reward for Jeroboam's own conduct.

views.label: A form of common grace exercised even toward persistently unfaithful rulers for the sake of the wider people
views.case: The text's explicit and pointed contrast, Jeroboam's personal evil against the LORD's compassion for the suffering people, may be read as an example of God graciously working through and even blessing flawed human authority for the sake of the broader population's relief, a pattern seen elsewhere when God uses imperfect or even pagan rulers to accomplish His purposes of care for the vulnerable.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the text explicitly refuses to credit Jeroboam's own righteousness for this success, instead locating its cause entirely in God's compassion and prior covenant commitment, a crucial corrective against equating national prosperity with divine approval of a ruler's character.

study.originalLanguages: Jehoash correctly diagnoses that Amaziah's lev, heart, has grown proud, a gaon, pride, following genuine victory that leads him to provoke a conflict costing him his own capital's chomah, wall, breached by the very rival he challenged. In deliberate contrast, Jeroboam II's striking success flows entirely from the LORD's determination that Israel's shem, name, would not yet be blotted out from under heaven, a mercy rooted purely in God's own prior word rather than any merit in this persistently idolatrous king.

typology.type: Pride following genuine victory leading to a self-inflicted, humbling downfall
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's consistent warning that pride precedes destruction, and God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, James 4:6, Proverbs 16:18.

typology.type: National blessing granted purely from divine compassion rather than merit
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of God's kindness intended to lead to repentance rather than presumption, Romans 2:4.

study.christ: Amaziah's proud, self-generated provocation of an unnecessary conflict after a genuine victory stands in sharp contrast to Christ, who, though possessing all authority in heaven and earth, refused to grasp at status or provoke unnecessary conflict, humbling Himself in obedience even to death on a cross, Philippians 2:6-8. Where Amaziah's pride led to Jerusalem's own wall being breached and his eventual assassination, Christ's humility led to His exaltation as Lord of all, showing that true and lasting strength is found not in self-assertion but in humble submission to the Father.
study.god: God grants real, sometimes remarkable success even to persistently unfaithful rulers, as with Jeroboam II, purely from compassion on a suffering people and faithfulness to His own prior word, refusing to let success be read as automatic proof of righteousness. He also allows pride that follows genuine blessing to work itself out toward a humbling fall, as with Amaziah, revealing a moral order in history that operates even when the proud cannot yet see it in their moment of triumph.

crossReferences.note: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall, the exact pattern displayed in Amaziah's provoked defeat.
Identifies the same prophet Jonah son of Amittai whose earlier word of restoration for Israel is fulfilled in this chapter, before his more famous mission to Nineveh.
God's kindness is meant to lead to repentance, a truth Jeroboam II's undeserved national prosperity should have prompted.
Christ's humble self-emptying stands in direct contrast to Amaziah's self-exalting pride after victory.

study.oneStory: Amaziah's proud overreach following a real victory, and Jeroboam II's undeserved national prosperity despite persistent idolatry, together testify that human success is never a simple measure of righteousness but reflects a deeper moral order under God's sovereign compassion and justice. This pattern anticipates the gospel truth that all blessing ultimately flows from grace rather than merit, fully and finally displayed in Christ's humble obedience rather than self-exalting human pride.

quiz.q: How did Amaziah handle the execution of his father's murderers, and what principle guided his decision?

options.text: He executed the murderers themselves but spared their children, following the Law of Moses that each person is to die for his own sin.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 record this decision and its explicit basis in the Law of Moses, Deuteronomy 24:16.

options.text: He executed both the murderers and all of their descendants.
options.note: The text explicitly states he spared the sons of the murderers, following the Law's principle of individual responsibility.

options.text: He pardoned the murderers entirely out of political weakness.
options.note: The murderers themselves were executed; only their children were spared according to the Law.

options.text: He took no action against his father's murderers at all.
options.note: The text explicitly records that he executed the servants who had murdered his father.

quiz.q: What warning did Jehoash of Israel give Amaziah before their confrontation, and how did Amaziah respond?

options.text: Jehoash warned that Amaziah's heart had become proud after defeating Edom and urged him to stay home rather than provoke a ruinous war, but Amaziah would not listen.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 record this exact warning through a parable and Amaziah's refusal to heed it.

options.text: Jehoash immediately agreed to Amaziah's proposed alliance.
options.note: Jehoash responded with a pointed parable warning against pride, not an agreement to ally.

options.text: Jehoash refused to respond to Amaziah at all.
options.note: Jehoash gave a detailed, specific response using the parable of the thistle and the cedar.

options.text: Amaziah heeded the warning and withdrew his challenge.
options.note: The text explicitly states Amaziah would not listen, leading directly to the battle at Beth-shemesh.

quiz.q: What was the outcome of the battle between Amaziah and Jehoash at Beth-shemesh?

options.text: Judah was routed, Amaziah was captured, and Jehoash breached Jerusalem's wall and plundered the temple and palace treasuries.
options.note: Verses 12 to 14 record this decisive and humiliating defeat for Judah.

options.text: Judah won a decisive victory and captured Jehoash.
options.note: The text records the opposite outcome; Judah was routed and Amaziah himself was captured.

options.text: The battle ended in a stalemate with no clear victor.
options.note: The text describes a clear and decisive rout of Judah's forces, not a stalemate.

options.text: No battle actually took place between the two kings.
options.note: The text explicitly describes the battle at Beth-shemesh and its outcome in detail.

quiz.q: How did Amaziah's reign ultimately end?

options.text: Conspirators plotted against him in Jerusalem, and though he fled to Lachish, he was pursued and killed there.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20 record this exact conspiracy, flight, and assassination.

options.text: He died peacefully after a long and celebrated reign.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a violent conspiracy and assassination, not a peaceful death.

options.text: He was killed in the battle against Jehoash at Beth-shemesh.
options.note: Amaziah survived the battle, living fifteen more years, verse 17, before being assassinated later by conspirators.

options.text: He successfully suppressed the conspiracy against him.
options.note: The conspirators succeeded in killing him at Lachish, not being suppressed by him.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD grant Jeroboam II such extensive territorial success despite his continued idolatry?

options.text: Because the LORD saw Israel's bitter affliction, had no one else to help them, and had determined not to blot out Israel's name from under heaven.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 give this exact, explicit reasoning grounded in divine compassion, not Jeroboam's righteousness.

options.text: Because Jeroboam II had abandoned Jeroboam I's idolatrous worship.
options.note: Verse 24 explicitly states he did not turn away from Jeroboam's sins, so his idolatry continued unchanged.

options.text: Because Israel's army had become larger than any surrounding nation.
options.note: The text attributes the success to divine compassion and covenant faithfulness, not simply Israel's military size.

options.text: The text gives no reason for Jeroboam's success.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 give a specific, explicit theological reason for this outcome.

questions: Amaziah's genuine victory over Edom fed a pride that led directly to a devastating defeat. What recent success in your own life carries the risk of feeding a pride that could lead you into unnecessary trouble?

Jehoash gave Amaziah wise counsel to stay home and enjoy his victory rather than provoke further conflict, but Amaziah would not listen. Where might you need to accept wise counsel to stop rather than push further into a fight you do not need to have?

Jeroboam II's success came purely from God's compassion for a suffering people, not from his own righteousness. Where might you be assuming that your own current blessings are proof of your good standing rather than simply God's undeserved mercy?
Amaziah's proud overreach ultimately cost him Jerusalem's own defenses and eventually his life. What boundary or defense in your own life might pride be causing you to needlessly expose or tear down?

This chapter shows that visible national or personal success is not automatically proof of righteousness before God. How do you currently discern the difference between God's blessing and God's mercy toward continued weakness or sin in your own life?

translationNotes.note: Sela, meaning rock, is widely identified with the later Nabataean city of Petra, and Amaziah's renaming it Joktheel marked his claimed possession of this significant fortified location.
Jehoash's parable of the thistle and the cedar mocks Amaziah's presumption in proposing an equal confrontation, comparing Judah's more modest strength to Israel's greater power, the cedar.

This is the same prophet Jonah son of Amittai known from the book of Jonah, confirming his ministry included this earlier, favorable prophetic word concerning Israel's territorial restoration under Jeroboam II.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 14:1-29. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
